profile.name: Rohan Adhav
profile.tagline: I build backend systems that stay fast under load, and I like proving it with numbers.

education.institute: D Y Patil College of Engineering, Pune

achievements: Ranked among the Top 1,500 candidates nationally in the Google Big Coding Challenge across India.
Solved 1,000+ DSA problems across LeetCode, GeeksforGeeks, Codeforces and CodeChef.
Winner — Innovative Tech Hackathon 2024; built a caching/query-optimization layer that cut API response time by 30%.

codingProfiles.summary: 1,000+ problems solved across four judges, with ratings that put me in the top bracket of competitive solvers on each platform.

experience.summary: Built and scaled backend REST APIs for a DSA practice platform, cutting query latency by 42%.
highlights: Reduced average query latency by 42% through compound indexing and pagination optimization.
Scaled endpoints to handle 10K+ concurrent problem-retrieval requests at sub-200ms response times.
Integrated the React.js frontend with backend services for the code editor, submission engine, and live dashboard.
Resolved production bugs through structured code reviews and QA feedback cycles.

detail.overview: ITJOBOX runs a DSA practice platform where users solve problems in a browser-based editor and see their submissions judged in near real time. I joined as an SDE intern to work on the backend that serves problems, holds submissions, and keeps the dashboard in sync with what's happening in the editor.
responsibilities: Designed and shipped REST endpoints for problem retrieval, submission tracking, and dashboard aggregation.
Added compound indexes on the most frequently filtered fields (difficulty, tags, company) and rebuilt pagination to use cursor-based queries instead of offset-based ones.
Wrote MongoDB aggregation pipelines to power leaderboard and progress views without pulling full collections into application memory.
Worked with QA to reproduce and fix production bugs, then added regression tests around the fixed paths.
impact: 42% cut in average query latency after the indexing and pagination rework.
Endpoints held up under 10K+ concurrent problem-retrieval requests at sub-200ms p95 response time.
Fewer repeat production bugs after regression coverage was added around fixed issues.
detail.learnings: This was my first time owning latency as a metric end-to-end — from writing the query, to indexing it correctly, to proving the improvement with load tests rather than assuming it. It also taught me how much of 'backend performance' is really about picking the right access pattern before writing any code.

experience.summary: Owned the full pipeline for a sensor time-series model: cleaning, feature engineering, training, and evaluation.
highlights: Preprocessed 50K+ time-series sensor samples, reducing noise by 28% with moving-average filtering and IQR-based outlier removal.
Engineered 15+ statistical and frequency-domain features, improving baseline regression R² by 0.31.
Owned the end-to-end model workflow from ingestion through training, tuning, evaluation, and documentation.
Benchmarked performance using RMSE, R², accuracy, and F1-score, and presented findings for the next iteration.

detail.overview: AROBIX AI works on applied ML for sensor data. My job was to take raw, noisy time-series sensor readings and turn them into a clean, feature-rich dataset that a regression model could actually learn from — then take the model through training and evaluation myself.
responsibilities: Cleaned 50K+ raw sensor samples using moving-average smoothing and IQR-based outlier removal.
Engineered 15+ statistical and frequency-domain features (rolling mean/variance, spectral energy, zero-crossing rate) from the cleaned signal.
Trained and tuned baseline regression models, iterating on feature sets based on validation performance.
Documented the pipeline and findings for the team to guide the next modeling iteration.
impact: 28% reduction in signal noise after preprocessing, measured against the raw baseline.
R² improved by 0.31 over the raw-data baseline after feature engineering.
Delivered a documented, reproducible pipeline that the team could hand off between iterations.
detail.learnings: This internship pushed me from 'run a model' to 'own a pipeline' — thinking about noise, feature validity, and evaluation metrics as a connected chain rather than separate steps.

projects.name: MindEdix
projects.tagline: A Redis-inspired in-memory key-value store with LRU eviction, a write-ahead log, and a live dashboard.

timeline.title: Core store
timeline.detail: HashMap-backed store with O(1) get/put and a doubly-linked list for LRU ordering.

timeline.title: Durability
timeline.detail: Added a write-ahead log with fsync so acknowledged writes survive a crash.

timeline.title: Concurrency
timeline.detail: Added synchronized multi-threaded access without serializing every request.

timeline.title: Dashboard
timeline.detail: Built a React dashboard for live hit/miss ratio, evictions, and memory usage.

docs.overview: MindEdix is an in-memory key-value store built from scratch in Java, in the spirit of Redis. It's meant to answer one question honestly: what does it actually take to make a cache fast, durable, and observable, rather than just 'a HashMap with a wrapper'.
docs.problem: Most educational key-value stores stop at 'HashMap + get/put'. That leaves out the three things that make a real cache hard: eviction under memory pressure, durability across crashes, and safe concurrent access — plus visibility into what the cache is actually doing at runtime.
docs.architecture: The core is a HashMap for O(1) lookups paired with a doubly-linked list that tracks access order for O(1) LRU eviction. Every write is appended to a write-ahead log before being acknowledged, so a crash can replay the log to rebuild state. A thread-safe layer around the core structure allows concurrent get/put without turning every operation into a single global lock. A Spring Boot layer exposes this over HTTP, and a React dashboard subscribes to live stats.
features: O(1) get/put via HashMap with O(1) LRU eviction using a doubly-linked list.
Write-ahead log with fsync durability — 100% crash recovery for acknowledged writes, verified with automated fault-injection tests.
Thread-safe concurrent access with fine-grained synchronization instead of a single global lock.
Live React dashboard showing hit/miss ratio, eviction events, and real-time memory usage.

challenges.title: Proving durability, not just claiming it
challenges.detail: It's easy to say 'the WAL guarantees durability' and hard to prove it. I built automated fault-injection tests that kill the process mid-write at different points and assert that every acknowledged write is recovered on restart.

challenges.title: LRU eviction without O(n) scans
challenges.detail: A naive 'find the least recently used key' scan is O(n). Pairing the HashMap with a doubly-linked list keeps both lookup and eviction at O(1), at the cost of extra bookkeeping on every access.

challenges.title: Concurrency without killing throughput
challenges.detail: A single lock around the whole store is correct but slow. I scoped synchronization tightly around the structures that actually need it, which is what let the store hit meaningful throughput in benchmarks.

results: 100% crash recovery for acknowledged writes across automated fault-injection tests.
Live dashboard used throughout development to catch eviction and memory regressions early.

projects.name: Distributed API Rate Limiter & Gateway Dashboard
projects.tagline: Token-bucket and sliding-window rate limiting with Redis-backed shared state across instances.

timeline.title: Single-instance limiter
timeline.detail: Implemented token-bucket and sliding-window algorithms against an in-memory store.

timeline.title: Centralized state
timeline.detail: Moved counters into Redis so limits hold correctly across multiple gateway instances.

timeline.title: Load testing
timeline.detail: Validated the design under 100K simulated requests across 3 instances.

timeline.title: Dashboard
timeline.detail: Built a React dashboard for traffic patterns, blocked IPs, and per-tier limits.

docs.overview: A rate limiter and API gateway dashboard built to survive the one failure mode that breaks most homemade rate limiters: running on more than one instance.
docs.problem: An in-memory rate limiter works perfectly on a single instance and silently breaks the moment you scale horizontally — each instance enforces its own limit, so a client can get several times the intended quota just by hitting different instances. That's the exact failure mode this project targets.
docs.architecture: Two limiting algorithms — token-bucket and sliding-window — are implemented against a shared Redis store instead of local memory, so every gateway instance reads and writes the same counters. A thin Express layer applies the limiter as middleware in front of the protected routes, and a React dashboard reads traffic and block events for visualization.
features: Token-bucket and sliding-window limiters with Redis-backed distributed state.
Handles 5,000 concurrent requests across 3 instances with under 5ms of added overhead.
React dashboard for real-time traffic patterns, blocked-IP visibility, and violation alerting.

challenges.title: The horizontal-scaling failure mode
challenges.detail: Naive in-memory limiters silently under-enforce limits once you run more than one instance. Centralizing counters in Redis fixes this, but it means every rate-limit check is now a network round trip that has to stay fast.

challenges.title: Keeping overhead under 5ms at scale
challenges.detail: Redis-backed checks add latency by design. I kept the added overhead under 5ms per request even at 5,000 concurrent requests across 3 instances by keeping the limiter logic in a single round trip using Lua scripting inside Redis rather than multiple sequential commands.

challenges.title: Validating correctness, not just speed
challenges.detail: A limiter that's fast but wrong is worse than one that's slow. I validated correctness with distributed load testing at 100,000 simulated requests to confirm the limiter enforced the same quota regardless of which instance served the request.

results: 5,000 concurrent requests handled across 3 instances with under 5ms of added overhead.
Correctness validated with 100,000 simulated requests in distributed load tests.
Dashboard gives operators live visibility into traffic, blocked IPs, and tier-based limits.

projects.name: Network Anomaly & DDoS Detection Engine
projects.tagline: Real-time packet analysis and anomaly detection to flag SYN floods and port scans.

timeline.title: Packet capture
timeline.detail: Captured and parsed raw TCP/IP/UDP packets with Scapy, decoding headers manually.

timeline.title: Feature extraction
timeline.detail: Built per-source-IP features: packet rates, SYN/ACK ratios, and port diversity.

timeline.title: Anomaly model
timeline.detail: Trained an Isolation Forest model alongside statistical thresholding for flagging.

timeline.title: Tuning & dashboard
timeline.detail: Tuned thresholds via precision/recall analysis and built a live flagging dashboard.

docs.overview: A detection engine that watches live network traffic and flags the two attack patterns that show up most in practice: SYN floods and port scans — built on hand-decoded packet headers rather than a black-box library.
docs.problem: Detecting a DDoS attack after the fact from logs is easy; detecting it while it's happening, from raw packets, with a low false-positive rate, is the actual hard problem. Most tutorials stop at 'use a library to sniff packets' — this project goes down to manual header decoding and real anomaly modeling.
docs.architecture: Scapy captures raw TCP/IP/UDP packets, and headers are decoded manually rather than relying on high-level helpers. A sliding window maintains rolling traffic statistics per source IP: packet rate, SYN/ACK ratio, and port diversity. These features feed an Isolation Forest model combined with statistical thresholding, and a live dashboard surfaces flagged IPs with timestamps.
features: Manual TCP/IP/UDP header decoding from raw packets captured with Scapy.
Per-source-IP sliding-window features: packet rate, SYN/ACK ratio, port diversity.
Isolation Forest anomaly detection combined with statistical thresholding.
Live dashboard showing flagged suspicious activity with timestamp and source IP.

challenges.title: Keeping false positives low
challenges.detail: A detector that cries wolf gets ignored. I ran precision/recall analysis over simulated attack traffic to tune thresholds so genuine SYN floods and port scans were flagged without drowning operators in noise.

challenges.title: Doing header decoding by hand
challenges.detail: Relying on Scapy's high-level packet objects hides exactly what's happening at the protocol level. Decoding TCP/IP/UDP headers manually meant I fully understood what each feature (like SYN/ACK ratio) actually represented before feeding it to a model.

challenges.title: Streaming stats without replaying all traffic
challenges.detail: Recomputing features from scratch for every packet doesn't scale. A sliding-window approach kept per-IP statistics current in real time without re-scanning historical traffic.

results: Successfully flagged simulated SYN flood and port-scan traffic in testing.
Threshold tuning via precision/recall analysis kept false-positive rates low.
Live dashboard surfaces flagged IPs with timestamps for operator review.
